  "opinion": "Stereotype threat in STEM refers to the phenomenon where individuals feel at risk of confirming a negative stereotype about their social group, which can negatively impact their performance and participation. As someone who has faced my own challenges with self-doubt throughout life due to depression, I've seen firsthand how anxiety can hold people back from reaching their potential.\n\nMy father's PhD in engineering makes me acutely aware of the intellectual capabilities running in our family while simultaneously highlighting the generational divide between him and myself. Though he was proud of his work at NASA, my own vocational diploma in automotive technology feels worlds away – yet ironically, perhaps that difference is what gives me insight.\n\nThe question isn't whether we should address stereotype threat; it's about recognizing how invisible forces can shape outcomes long before they're visible results. Every time I see young women or minorities hesitant to pursue STEM careers because of fear-based performance pressure, it reminds me of my own internal battles with depression and imposter syndrome.\n\nWe need strategies that focus on reducing evaluative concerns rather than just boosting confidence. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
Stereotype threat in STEM refers to the phenomenon where individuals feel at risk of confirming a negative stereotype about their social group, which can negatively impact their performance and participation. As someone who has faced my own challenges with self-doubt throughout life due to depression, I've seen firsthand how anxiety can hold people back from reaching their potential.

My father's PhD in engineering makes me acutely aware of the intellectual capabilities running in our family while simultaneously highlighting the generational divide between him and myself. Though he was proud of his work at NASA, my own vocational diploma in automotive technology feels worlds away – yet ironically, perhaps that difference is what gives me insight.

The question isn't whether we should address stereotype threat; it's about recognizing how invisible forces can shape outcomes long before they're visible results. Every time I see young women or minorities hesitant to pursue STEM careers because of fear-based performance pressure, it reminds me of my own internal battles with depression and imposter syndrome.

We need strategies that focus on reducing evaluative concerns rather than just boosting confidence. Maybe mentorship programs pairing older workers like myself (who understands failure through grit) with vulnerable groups could be powerful tools.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I'll structure this by first defining stereotype threat, then drawing from personal experiences dealing with self-doubt due to depression while referencing my father's academic background to show how stereotypes can manifest even across generations. I'll discuss why it should be addressed and suggest practical solutions based on overcoming evaluative anxiety.
